source_title: Anchor materials: all-suture vs PEEK vs biocomposite
topic: Anchor materials: all-suture vs PEEK vs biocomposite
summary: The three dominant anchor material classes are all-suture (soft) anchors, PEEK (permanent polymer), and biocomposite (resorbable, e.g., PLLA + calcium-based fillers). A network meta-analysis found PEEK and all-suture anchors had the highest ultimate failure loads, metal intermediate, and biocomposite the lowest, with no meaningful stiffness differences; failure modes differ by type (all-suture more prone to pull-out, biocomposite to eyelet breakage, metal to suture cutting). Results are sensitive to bone mineral density. All three companies offer anchors in all three material classes, so material is often a surgeon-preference and bone-quality decision rather than a clear winner.
arthrex_position: Broad material menu: BioComposite (and PLLA) and nonabsorbable PEEK SwiveLock/SutureTak/PushLock anchor bodies, plus all-suture FiberTak soft anchors (e.g., 2.6 FiberTak RC). Arthrex emphasizes BioComposite for the SpeedBridge medial/lateral rows and FiberTak for low-bone-removal soft-anchor fixation.
stryker_position: Stryker leans into PEEK for its knotless anchors (ReelX STT all-PEEK body, Omega PEEK eyelet) and also offers biocomposite (Citrelock/CrossFT biocomposite) options. The PEEK eyelet is marketed to reduce suture abrasion.
smithnephew_position: Smith & Nephew differentiates on its REGENESORB biocomposite (designed to be replaced by bone within ~24 months) and the open-architecture HEALICOIL coil that lets marrow/stem cells reach the repair; it also offers HEALICOIL KNOTLESS PEEK and all-suture (Q-FIX) options.
rep_talk_track: A Stryker rep can fairly cite that PEEK anchors top the biomechanical failure-load rankings and resist suture abrasion. An S&N rep counters that REGENESORB plus open architecture is about long-term bone ingrowth and leaving less permanent foreign material, trading a bit of peak failure load for biology. An Arthrex rep says 'we let you pick - BioComposite, PEEK, PLLA, or all-suture FiberTak - per bone quality.' The honest caveat: biocomposite tends to rank lowest on raw failure load, all-suture removes the least bone but pulls out more often, and the differences are bone-density-dependent. There is no RCT showing one material yields better healed-cuff clinical outcomes.
evidence: Network meta-analysis (Yang et al., 2023): SUCRA ultimate failure load PEEK 81.9% > all-suture 76.5% > metal 38.7% > biocomposite 3.0%; no significant stiffness differences; all-suture more anchor pull-out, biocomposite more eyelet breakage; rankings shift with bone mineral density.
RCT (Arthroscopy): open-construct PEEK anchor showed better 6-month bone ingrowth than a non-vented biocomposite anchor.
Arthrex product pages confirm SwiveLock available in BioComposite, PLLA, PEEK; FiberTak is the all-suture line. Stryker confirms ReelX is PEEK. S&N confirms HEALICOIL uses REGENESORB biocomposite.
text:
Anchor materials: all-suture vs PEEK vs biocomposite

The three dominant anchor material classes are all-suture (soft) anchors, PEEK (permanent polymer), and biocomposite (resorbable, e.g., PLLA + calcium-based fillers). A network meta-analysis found PEEK and all-suture anchors had the highest ultimate failure loads, metal intermediate, and biocomposite the lowest, with no meaningful stiffness differences; failure modes differ by type (all-suture more prone to pull-out, biocomposite to eyelet breakage, metal to suture cutting). Results are sensitive to bone mineral density. All three companies offer anchors in all three material classes, so material is often a surgeon-preference and bone-quality decision rather than a clear winner.

Arthrex: Broad material menu: BioComposite (and PLLA) and nonabsorbable PEEK SwiveLock/SutureTak/PushLock anchor bodies, plus all-suture FiberTak soft anchors (e.g., 2.6 FiberTak RC). Arthrex emphasizes BioComposite for the SpeedBridge medial/lateral rows and FiberTak for low-bone-removal soft-anchor fixation.
Stryker: Stryker leans into PEEK for its knotless anchors (ReelX STT all-PEEK body, Omega PEEK eyelet) and also offers biocomposite (Citrelock/CrossFT biocomposite) options. The PEEK eyelet is marketed to reduce suture abrasion.
Smith & Nephew: Smith & Nephew differentiates on its REGENESORB biocomposite (designed to be replaced by bone within ~24 months) and the open-architecture HEALICOIL coil that lets marrow/stem cells reach the repair; it also offers HEALICOIL KNOTLESS PEEK and all-suture (Q-FIX) options.

Rep positioning: A Stryker rep can fairly cite that PEEK anchors top the biomechanical failure-load rankings and resist suture abrasion. An S&N rep counters that REGENESORB plus open architecture is about long-term bone ingrowth and leaving less permanent foreign material, trading a bit of peak failure load for biology. An Arthrex rep says 'we let you pick - BioComposite, PEEK, PLLA, or all-suture FiberTak - per bone quality.' The honest caveat: biocomposite tends to rank lowest on raw failure load, all-suture removes the least bone but pulls out more often, and the differences are bone-density-dependent. There is no RCT showing one material yields better healed-cuff clinical outcomes.

Evidence: (1) Network meta-analysis (Yang et al., 2023): SUCRA ultimate failure load PEEK 81.9% > all-suture 76.5% > metal 38.7% > biocomposite 3.0%; no significant stiffness differences; all-suture more anchor pull-out, biocomposite more eyelet breakage; rankings shift with bone mineral density. (2) RCT (Arthroscopy): open-construct PEEK anchor showed better 6-month bone ingrowth than a non-vented biocomposite anchor. (3) Arthrex product pages confirm SwiveLock available in BioComposite, PLLA, PEEK; FiberTak is the all-suture line. Stryker confirms ReelX is PEEK. S&N confirms HEALICOIL uses REGENESORB biocomposite.
